schools.summary: The sealed book — 'in the right hand of him that sat on the throne' (on the open palm, the emblem of sovereign power, held forth to give to one worthy); 'written within and on the backside' (covered with writing on both sides, complete, nothing wanting, a vast content); 'sealed with seven seals' (perfectly secured and hidden until rightfully opened); taken as the book of God's sealed counsels and decrees, the unfolding of his purposes, the 'things which must be hereafter.'
Victorinus and Bede: the book signifies holy Scripture, especially the Old Testament given into Christ's hands and opened through his Incarnation — the Old Testament without, the New within.

debates.question: What is the book sealed with seven seals?

sides.position: the book of God's sealed counsels and decrees — his providential and judicial purposes, the 'things which must be hereafter'
holy Scripture, especially the Old Testament given into Christ's hands and opened through the Incarnation

themes: a book in the right hand
written within and on the backside

schools.summary: The great challenge — 'a strong angel' (of mighty power, whose loud voice could carry to creation's farthest bounds, Rev 10:1; the magnitude of the question requiring a herald of corresponding strength); 'with a loud voice' (a universal summons that every creature might hear); 'who is worthy to open the book, and to loose the seals?' (not merely who is strong enough but who is morally worthy, having the fitness and right, Ellicott, to break the seals and disclose God's hidden counsels); a quest through the whole universe for one qualified.

themes: to open the book and loose the seals

schools.summary: The failure of all creation — 'no man in heaven, nor in earth, neither under the earth' (better 'no one,' the three regions naming the whole created universe — angels and saints, the living, the dead, Phil 2:10); none 'able to open the book, neither to look thereon' (not one worthy even to take and gaze into it); the highest angels cannot penetrate God's sealed decrees, no man, spirit, or power above or below can fathom his counsels (Clarke); the universal failure magnifying the worthiness of the one who can, preparing for the Lamb.

themes: no man in heaven nor earth

schools.summary: The seer's tears — 'I wept much' (eklaion poly, imperfect, continued even loud weeping, a flood of emotion); the cause 'because no man was found worthy to open and to read the book' (the unveiling of God's purposes seeming about to remain forever sealed, the book that promised the future appearing closed); not a failure of faith but a tender, large-hearted grief (Ellicott, Wesley: the Revelation not written, nor understood, without tears); the emphatic 'I,' the seer not ashamed to record his weeping; the tears setting off the joy to come.

The prevailing Lion announced — 'Weep not' (a representative of the redeemed staying John's tears, the deadlock broken); 'the Lion of the tribe of Juda' (from Jacob's blessing, Gen 49:9, Christ the royal Lion sprung from Judah, mighty and victorious, the promised king); 'the Root of David' (both David's descendant/shoot and the root from which David sprang, David's son and Lord); 'hath prevailed/conquered to open the book' (Christ overcoming by life, death, and resurrection, his victory the ground of his worthiness); announced as Lion, appearing as slain Lamb.

themes: the Lion of the tribe of Juda
hath prevailed to open the book

schools.summary: The slain yet living Lamb — the great paradox: announced as Lion, John beholds 'a Lamb as it had been slain' (the symbol of meekness and sacrifice, bearing the marks of slaughter yet alive and standing, the conquering king the crucified, risen victim); 'in the midst of the throne' (sharing God's very throne); 'as it had been slain' (he stands, lies no more, yet bears forever the tokens of his death, Wesley); 'seven horns' (perfect power and dominion) and 'seven eyes, which are the seven Spirits of God' (the Spirit's fulness, all-seeing omniscience); the victory won by the Lamb's death.

themes: a Lamb as it had been slain
in the midst of the throne

schools.summary: The Lamb taking the book — 'he came' (drawing near to the throne, the very seat of God, which no mere creature without a mediator could do); 'took the book out of the right hand of him that sat upon the throne' (received the sealed scroll from God's sovereign hand; the perfect tense vivid, 'he has taken it,' Robertson); the worthy one alone fit to receive God's counsels, with Jesus alone all the mysteries of God (Clarke, John 1:18); Psalm 2:8 'Ask of me' gloriously fulfilled (Wesley), the Father committing the book of his purposes to the Son's hand.

themes: he came and took the book
of him that sat on the throne

schools.summary: Heaven's worship of the Lamb — 'the four beasts and four and twenty elders fell down before the Lamb' (the living creatures and redeemed elders, who worshipped the Enthroned in ch4, now prostrate before the Lamb; the same adoration given to God given to the Lamb, a testimony to his deity); 'harps' (instruments of joyful praise for the coming song); 'golden vials full of odours, which are the prayers of saints' (golden censers of fragrant incense, the prayers of God's people rising before the throne, precious and accepted, not one lost).

The new song of redemption — 'a new song' (new because it celebrates a new finished work, redemption accomplished, that could not be sung until the Lamb was slain, Rev 14:3); 'Thou art worthy to take the book' (the worthiness the angel sought now owned in the Lamb); the ground differing from ch4's Creator-praise — 'for thou wast slain' (worthiness resting on his sacrificial death); 'hast redeemed us to God by thy blood' (purchasing a people at the price of his blood); 'out of every kindred, tongue, people, and nation' (the redeemed gathered from all races, knowing no national bounds).

themes: hast redeemed us by thy blood
out of every kindred and tongue

schools.summary: The dignity of the redeemed — 'hast made us unto our God kings and priests' (a royal-priesthood, drawing on Ex 19:6; Rev 1:6; 1 Pet 2:9; in Christ believers made kings sharing his dominion and priests with access to God and spiritual sacrifices, exercised toward and for God); 'we shall reign on the earth' (the redeemed destined to reign, referred to the present spiritual reign, the future reign of the saints, or the new earth); the redeemed speaking of themselves in the third person out of deep self-abasement (Wesley); ransomed sinners exalted to royalty.

themes: we shall reign on the earth

schools.summary: The innumerable angelic chorus — 'the voice of many angels round about the throne, and the beasts, and the elders' (a vaster outer circle beyond the inner ring of creatures and elders, heaven's worship spreading in widening rings); 'ten thousand times ten thousand, and thousands of thousands' (myriads of myriads, an uncountable multitude, echoing Dan 7:10); the angels answering and amplifying the redeemed's new song antiphonally; Wesley's note that the redeemed are placed nearer God than the angels — the ransomed in the inner circle, the angels in the outer; the angels rejoicing in man's redemption.

The sevenfold doxology to the Lamb — 'with a loud voice' (the countless angels proclaiming for all to hear); 'Worthy is the Lamb that was slain' (his sacrificial death the ground, the angels, though not redeemed, owning his desert); the sevenfold tribute 'power, riches, wisdom, strength, honour, glory, and blessing' (the number of completeness, the fulness of all praise — omnipotence, beneficence, omniscience, might, reputation, splendour, thanksgiving, Clarke); the angels saying 'worthy is the Lamb' (third person, less nearly related than the redeemed who said 'worthy art thou,' Wesley); answering the seven seals.

themes: Worthy is the Lamb that was slain

schools.summary: The universal chorus — 'every creature which is in heaven, and on the earth, and under the earth, and in the sea' (the four regions of the universe, every created thing, the circles of worship widening from creatures to elders to angels to all creation); 'Blessing, and honour, and glory, and power' (a fourfold tribute agreeing with the four regions, Wesley); 'unto him that sitteth on the throne, and unto the Lamb' (God and the Lamb praised together on equal terms, a clear testimony to the Lamb's deity); 'for ever and ever' (everlasting praise); the chorus of the universe (Ellicott).

themes: every creature in heaven and earth
him that sitteth on the throne, and the Lamb

schools.summary: The sealing of the worship — 'the four beasts said, Amen' (the four living creatures, who began the praise in ch4, setting their seal of assent to the universal ascription, affirming all creation has said is true and worthy); 'the four and twenty elders fell down and worshipped him that liveth for ever and ever' (the redeemed closing the service, as they opened it, with silent prostrate adoration of the eternal God and the Lamb); the symmetry — the song swelling outward through angels and all creation, returning to the inner circle's wordless worship; assent and adoration the proper response.

themes: the elders fell down and worshipped
him that liveth for ever and ever
